Philadelphia Urban Heat Inequity ML Analysis · 30,864 grid cells · 100m resolution

Research Overview
As urban populations concentrate and climate change intensifies, cities are becoming increasingly unequal thermal environments. Urban heat islands are not simply a consequence of physical urbanization — they are socially patterned through decades of discriminatory investment and disinvestment. Historically redlined neighborhoods consistently experience higher surface temperatures, lower tree canopy, and reduced access to cooling infrastructure. Yet while existing research documents where these inequities occur and who is affected, fewer studies examine the spatial mechanisms through which historical disadvantage is translated into present-day thermal exposure. This project operationalizes those mechanisms at 100m grid resolution using machine learning — and uses systematic model failures as diagnostic signals for what 2D satellite features cannot see.
Research Questions
RQ1 Which built-environment and socio-demographic features most strongly predict land surface temperature at 100m grid resolution across Philadelphia, and how do their relative contributions compare to linear baselines?
RQ2 Where do model residuals reveal spatial exceptions to citywide heat patterns — grids that are unexpectedly hot or cool given their 2D features — and do these exceptions cluster along lines of historical disinvestment?
RQ3 What 3D urban morphology features — Sky View Factor, canyon geometry, building height — can explain the residual anomalies that 2D satellite features cannot capture, motivating a computer vision extension?
Study Design
30,864
100m × 100m grid cells
9
features across 5 sources
R²=0.909
RF 5-fold spatial CV
2,656
spatial anomalies (±1.5σ)
Target: LST (°C) from USGS Landsat Collection 2, averaged over 2021–2025 summer scenes. Evaluation uses 5-fold spatial block cross-validation — not random splits — to provide honest generalization estimates given spatial autocorrelation among neighboring cells.
Data Sources
LST TARGET
USGS Landsat Collection 2 Level-2 thermal imagery · 2021–2025 summer scenes averaged
LAND COVER
MRLC Annual NLCD 2024 · impervious surface %, tree canopy % per-grid means
BUILT ENV.
OpenDataPhilly building footprints 2025 · OSM road network · spatially intersected
DEMOGRAPHICS
ACS 5-Year Estimates 2020–2024 · census tract level joined in ArcGIS Pro
SPATIAL CONTEXT
HOLC Redlining Maps 1937 · Mapping Inequality Project · OSM land use
Positioning in the Literature
Urban heat inequity & redlining
Socially vulnerable neighborhoods experience disproportionately higher thermal exposure — a pattern linked to land cover composition and socioeconomic characteristics (Harlan et al., 2006). Hoffman et al. (2020) show that formerly redlined areas are warmer in 94% of 108 US cities; Wilson (2020) connects this to long-term infrastructure deprivation including tree canopy and park investment. Hsu et al. (2021) document disproportionate UHI exposure at population scale across US cities.
Hoffman et al. 2020 · Wilson 2020 · Hsu et al. 2021
Spatial mechanisms approach
Fewer studies examine how historical disadvantage is translated into thermal exposure through spatial configurations. Heat inequity is sensitive not only to amount of green infrastructure but also to its spatial configuration — fragmentation, clustering, edge complexity — beyond mean-NDVI (Chen, 2024; Terfa et al., 2020). This mechanism-oriented framing connects inequity patterns to actionable spatial levers for planners.
Hashemi & Adib 2024 · Quang et al. 2025 · Chen 2024
3D morphology gap
Sky View Factor and canyon geometry significantly influence LST at sub-100m scales but are absent from 2D satellite feature sets. Street View imagery + deep learning (SegFormer) can extract building view factor and green view index at city scale — the CV extension this project motivates, directly addressing the Center City cool anomaly (−4°C despite 81% impervious).
Oke 1981 · Li et al. 2024, Building and Environment · Xu et al. 2024 · Chen et al. 2025
Key References
Inequity Harlan et al. (2006). Neighborhood microclimates and vulnerability to heat stress. Social Science & Medicine, 63(11), 2847–2863. doi:10.1016/j.socscimed.2006.07.030

Redlining Wilson, B. (2020). Urban Heat Management and the Legacy of Redlining. Journal of the American Planning Association, 86(4), 443–457. doi:10.1080/01944363.2020.1759127
Inequity Hsu et al. (2021). Disproportionate exposure to urban heat island intensity across major US cities. Nature Communications, 12, 2721. doi:10.1038/s41467-021-22799-5
Philly Schinasi et al. (2022). Associations Between Historical Redlining and Present-Day Heat Vulnerability Housing and Land Cover Characteristics in Philadelphia, PA. Journal of Urban Health, 99, 397–410. doi:10.1007/s11524-021-00602-6
Canopy Walker et al. (2024). Legacies of redlining lead to unequal cooling effects of urban tree canopy. Landscape and Urban Planning, 245, 105009. doi:10.1016/j.landurbplan.2024.105009
Trees Locke et al. (2021). Residential housing segregation and urban tree canopy in 37 US Cities. npj Urban Sustainability, 1, 15. doi:10.1038/s42949-021-00022-0
ML / UHI Tanoori et al. (2024). Machine Learning for Urban Heat Island (UHI) Analysis: Predicting Land Surface Temperature (LST) in Urban Environments. Urban Climate, 55, 101962. doi:10.1016/j.uclim.2024.101962
SVF Oke, T.R. (1981). Canyon geometry and the nocturnal urban heat island: comparison of scale model and field observations. Journal of Climatology, 1(3), 237–254. doi:10.1002/joc.3370010304
CV / SVI Li et al. (2024). Evaluating the seasonal effects of building form and street view indicators on street-level land surface temperature using random forest regression. Building and Environment, 249, 111013. doi:10.1016/j.buildenv.2023.111013
CV / SVF Xu et al. (2024). Online Street View-Based Approach for Sky View Factor Estimation. Applied Sciences, 14(5), 2133. doi:10.3390/app14052133
CV / Equity Chen et al. (2025). Assessing the influence of urban scene characteristics on urban heat island: An interpretable ML approach in New York City. Urban Climate. ScienceDirect (in press) — SVI K-means clustering (sky/tree/building/road) + RF + SHAP; closest methodological parallel to this project.
EDA & Distributions
Nine features across 30,864 grid cells. Land cover variables (impervious %, canopy %) derived from NLCD 2024 raster; building/road density from vector intersection with OpenDataPhilly and OSM; demographics from ACS 5-year estimates joined at census-tract level.
Correlation Analysis
Pearson correlations with Mean LST. Impervious surface shows the strongest positive correlation (+0.869); tree canopy the strongest negative (−0.713). The poverty rate coefficient (−0.74 in linear model) reflects spatial confounding — lower-income communities in Philadelphia are not uniformly in the hottest urban cores.
Pearson r with LST
Feature Correlation Matrix
Scatter Plots
Each point is a 100m grid cell. The consistent improvement from linear to nonlinear models (R²: 0.790 → 0.909) reflects threshold effects and conditional interactions — for instance, the cooling effect of canopy intensifies at higher coverage levels, and impervious surface heating interacts with building density in ways a linear model cannot express.
ML Model Comparison
Baseline experiments (WEKA, 80/20 split) confirmed a performance gradient — Linear Regression → REPTree → Random Forest — indicating nonlinear feature interactions. RF was selected as the primary model. 5-fold spatial cross-validation (block-based, not random) provides honest generalization estimates accounting for spatial autocorrelation.
R² by Model (5-fold spatial CV)
RF Feature Importance
Key Observations — Model Performance
Nonlinearity confirmed. The consistent performance gradient (correlation: 0.895 → 0.937 → 0.956) from linear to tree to ensemble models indicates LST prediction benefits substantially from nonlinear feature interactions — threshold effects and conditional relationships among variables not expressible by linear regression.
Consistent RMSE/MAE ratio ≈ 1.38. This ratio is stable across all three models, pointing to a subset of grid cells that produce large errors regardless of model complexity. This is a data representation issue — atypical microenvironments not captured by the current 2D feature set — and is the direct motivation for residual-based anomaly detection.
Spatial CV vs. random split. 5-fold spatial block CV gives a more honest performance estimate than the baseline random split (R²=0.956 correlation), as neighboring cells share spatial autocorrelation that inflates random-split scores.
Key Observations — Feature Coefficients
Impervious surface dominates (+10.82). Strongest positive predictor at 78.9% RF importance. The magnitude gap between impervious (+10.82) and canopy (−3.34) suggests an asymmetry: urban heating from paving is harder to offset through greening alone at current canopy levels.
Building coverage (+2.44) surprisingly weak. Likely reflects buildings' partial shadow effect partially offsetting their thermal mass contribution — a 3D morphology effect that 2D features cannot cleanly resolve.
% Below poverty (−0.74) ⚠ counterintuitive. Higher poverty rate associated with slightly lower LST in the linear model — this is implausible as a direct physical mechanism. More likely: spatial confounding. Lower-income communities in Philadelphia are not uniformly located in the hottest urban cores, and the coefficient absorbs variance from correlated built-environment patterns. The residual map directly addresses this: hot anomalies in HOLC D zones reveal the hidden heat burden this coefficient obscures.
RF Residual Map · Spatial Anomaly Detection

Spatial Analysis · click to highlight zone
① Structural Cool Island
−3.7°C
Mt Airy / Chestnut Hill · HOLC A/B
Chestnut Hill was graded HOLC "A" in 1937, directing decades of mortgage investment that funded tree planting and park maintenance. Today the area has ~60% tree canopy — highest in the city — and borders Wissahickon Valley Park (2,042 acres), which creates a topographic cooling corridor.
Mt Airy became one of the first intentionally racially integrated neighborhoods in the US in the 1950s, a stability that preserved its green infrastructure through an era when other Philadelphia neighborhoods lost theirs.
Model over-predicts LST here: valley topography and mature tree age are invisible to 2D satellite features — this is the structural "green privilege" baseline.
Walker et al. (2024) Landscape & Urban Planning · Locke et al. (2021) npj Urban Sustainability · Schinasi et al. (2022) J. Urban Health
② Reinvestment Zone
−3.7°C
West Philadelphia · HOLC C/D
Known historically as the "Black Bottom," this area was graded HOLC D in 1937. In the 1960s, the West Philadelphia Corporation — led by Penn as majority shareholder, with Drexel as junior partner — used eminent domain to clear the neighborhood for the University City Science Center. A total of 2,653 people were displaced; 78% were Black (Black Bottom Tribe; Puckett, Penn Collaborative History). Since the 1990s, Penn and Drexel have invested over $4.5B in University City — adding Drexel Square, Penn Park, and street trees — while the surrounding area remains majority minority (68%) with 21% poverty.
The result is a spatially uneven cooling signal: new high-albedo buildings and 2010s tree planting produce a −3.7°C model residual despite structural socioeconomic disadvantage remaining high.
Most analytically interesting anomaly type: historical disinvestment + recent physical cooling — the reinvestment signal is invisible to 2D features trained on prior land cover.
Black Bottom Tribe · Puckett — Penn Collaborative History · Wolf-Powers, L. (2023) University City: History, Race, and Community · Hoffman et al. (2020) Climate
③ Urban Canyon Effect
−4.0°C
Center City · HOLC A/B
Philadelphia's street grid — originally 50–100ft wide — is now flanked by towers up to 1,113ft (Comcast Technology Center, 2018). Deep H/W ratios create street canyons with low Sky View Factor (SVF), trapping morning/evening shadow and reducing net shortwave radiation absorbed at the surface — producing measurable daytime LST cooling despite 81% impervious surface and ~2% canopy.
This is a well-documented urban climatology phenomenon (Oke 1981): nocturnal heat storage increases with lower SVF, but daytime satellite-derived LST can be lower in deep canyons because shaded surfaces dominate the thermal signal.
Primary CV target. SVF and canyon geometry are exactly what street-level imagery can measure — and exactly what 2D satellite features cannot.
Oke (1981) J. Climatology · Xu et al. (2024) Applied Sciences · Li et al. (2024) Building and Environment
④ Heat Inequity Core
+4.4°C
Kensington / N. Philly · HOLC D
Kensington was Philadelphia's textile heartland — by 1882 the city had over 60,000 textile workers in ~1,000 firms, heavily concentrated here. Deindustrialization from the 1950s left citywide approximately 40,000 vacant lots (City of Philadelphia). Today Kensington has median household income $28,368 (ACS 2020), ~7% tree canopy, and among the highest rates of AC-less households in the city.
During July 6–14, 1993, Philadelphia's medical examiners determined 118 deaths were heat-related. ME Haresh Mirchandani's expanded methodology was later validated by NOAA and CDC as the new national standard. Most victims were elderly people living alone without AC.
228 hot anomaly grids, minority 66%, HOLC D. Model under-predicts: building material quality and rooftop albedo amplify heat beyond what impervious surface alone captures.
CDC/MMWR (1994) · Mirchandani et al. (1996) Am J Forensic Med Pathol · City of Philadelphia Vacant Lot Program · Schinasi et al. (2022) J. Urban Health
⑤ Feature Representation Gap
+7.9°C
NE Philadelphia · Torresdale / Holmesburg
⚠ Hypothesis — land use shapefile validation pending
61 grids: building coverage ~46%, NLCD impervious ~4.5%, yet LST = 45.3°C. Model predicts 37.3°C. Gap = +7.9°C — largest systematic residual in dataset.
Internal evidence for large isolated structures not captured by NLCD:
① Road density median = 0 m/grid vs 107m for normal NE grids
② Building/imperv divergence systematic — r=0.68 citywide, ~0 here
③ 97% concentrated near I-95 logistics corridor
NLCD imperv undercounts large-rooftop commercial structures in suburban zones. The observed LST is real (Landsat) — the model feature is the gap.
Xian et al. (2011) Remote Sensing of Environment
Residual Distribution
Anomaly Summary
Future Directions & Policy Implications
R²=0.909
RF 5-fold spatial CV
Impervious surface explains 78.9% of RF importance. Nonlinear ensemble methods capture threshold effects invisible to linear regression (R²=0.790).
1,905
Unexpectedly hot grids
Concentrated in Kensington / North Philly (HOLC D, minority 66%). Hidden heat burden: building material quality, rooftop albedo, and AC absence amplify exposure beyond impervious surface alone.
751
Unexpectedly cool grids
Three distinct mechanisms: structural green privilege (NW corridor), university reinvestment signal (West Philly), and urban canyon shade (Center City, −4°C despite 81% impervious).
Direction 1
CV Extension — 3D Morphology
Sky View Factor, building height, and canyon geometry explain the Center City anomaly that 2D features cannot. Next step: SegFormer semantic segmentation on Google Street View imagery to extract Building View Factor and Green View Index at city scale, then augment the RF model and measure incremental R² gain.
Li et al. 2024, Building and Environment · Xu et al. 2024, Applied Sciences
Direction 2
Algorithmic Fairness
Do prediction errors cluster systematically in socio-economically marginalized neighborhoods? The confounded poverty coefficient (−0.74) and hot anomaly spatial distribution suggest unequal model representational capacity. Formal fairness audit: compare RMSE stratified by HOLC grade, income quartile, and minority percentage.
Hsu et al. 2021 · Chen et al. 2025, Urban Climate
Direction 3
From Correlation to Causality
ML identifies association, not causation. A counterfactual framework — "what would happen if canopy cover in Kensington increased by 15%?" — requires integration with urban simulation tools or causal inference methods (e.g., difference-in-differences using tree planting program rollout data).
Philly Tree Plan 2022 · TreePhilly program data
Priority Interventions by Anomaly Zone
Zone Anomaly Root cause Intervention priority CV extension role
Kensington / N. Philly +4.4°C hot Low canopy (7%), aging industrial stock, no AC Street tree planting equity, cool roof subsidies, cooling centers Measure rooftop albedo + street shade via SVI
Mt Airy / Chestnut Hill −3.7°C cool HOLC A/B, 60% canopy, Wissahickon valley Benchmark for citywide tree equity programs Quantify valley topography cooling via 3D data
West Philadelphia −3.7°C cool Penn+Drexel $4.5B reinvestment, new street trees Anti-displacement; ensure cooling reaches remaining low-income residents Track new vs legacy building albedo via SVI
Center City −4.0°C cool Urban canyon shade, low SVF (1,113ft towers) Quantify SVF contribution to thermal comfort Primary CV validation target — measure SVF directly
Urban Vertical Column Analysis — Street-Level Morphology
Semantic segmentation of 300 Google Street View images (SegFormer-B0, ADE20K) across 75 anomaly grids. Each column shows the pixel composition of the street-level view and derived morphology indicators for each scenario.
Pixel classes
Sky (SVF)
Vegetation (GVI)
Building (BVF)
Road (RVF)
Other
Key ecological findings
① ② Vegetation cooling mechanisms
Mt Airy and West Philly share the highest GVI (0.40), but differ in mechanism. Mt Airy's Canopy Height Proxy (0.34) signals mature tall trees driving evapotranspiration and solar shading. West Philly's extreme Veg-Sky ratio (16.6) reflects dense street tree planting associated with recent reinvestment.
③ Street canyon architectural shading
Center City shows near-zero GVI but unexpectedly cool temperatures. Canyon ratio peaks at 1392 — buildings so tall relative to sky opening that they shade each other. Cooling here is architectural, not ecological: mutual building shading reduces ground-level solar absorption without vegetation.
④ Open-sky thermal exposure
Kensington has the highest SVF (0.34) in the dataset — maximum sky openness means full, unobstructed solar loading. With near-zero canopy and low building enclosure, these industrial grids receive more direct radiation than anywhere else. This dimension is invisible to 2D satellite imagery.
The ML model's structural blind spot
SVF shows the strongest correlation with model residuals (r = +0.28). The existing Random Forest detects impervious surfaces from above, but cannot perceive whether those surfaces are shaded or exposed. Street-level sky openness is the primary dimension that satellite data systematically misses.
Computer Vision Analysis — Street-Level Urban Morphology
Computer vision pipeline
75 anomaly grids sampled across 5 scenarios. Each grid photographed in 4 cardinal directions (N / E / S / W) via Google Street View API → 300 images total. Indicators averaged across 4 directions per grid.
01
Anomaly detection
RF residuals > ±2.93°C
02
Stratified sampling
75 grids · 5 scenarios
03
Street View API
75 grids × 4 dirs = 300 images
04
SegFormer-B0
ADE20K · 150 classes
05
Indicator extraction
7 indicators · avg per grid
Sampling design
Within each scenario, grids ranked by |residual|. Top grids selected with ≥330 m minimum spacing to avoid spatial redundancy.

4 directions per grid (N/E/S/W, FOV=90°) capture the full 360° street environment, then averaged to one grid-level value per indicator.
Segmentation model
SegFormer-B0
Pre-trained on ADE20K (150 classes). Transformer encoder + MLP decoder. 640×640 px input, bilinear upsampling to original resolution. Run on NVIDIA T4 (Google Colab). 300/300 images processed successfully.
nvidia/segformer-b0-finetuned-ade-512-512
ADE20K class mapping
Sky → class 2
Vegetation → 4, 9, 17, 61, 66, 96, 128
Building → 0, 1, 25, 48, 84
Road → 6, 11, 13, 52, 53
Other → remaining 135 classes
Example: street view → segmentation → indicators
One directional image shown per grid. Each grid's final indicators are the mean of all 4 directions (N/E/S/W).

Seven morphological indicators — definition and ecological significance
GVI — Green View Index
Vegetation pixel fraction. Captures street-level canopy invisible to satellite canopy maps. Primary driver of evapotranspiration cooling.
SVF — Sky View Factor
Sky pixel fraction. Governs solar exposure and longwave emission. Strongest correlation with model residuals (r = +0.28). The primary satellite blind spot.
BVF — Building View Factor
Building-surface pixel fraction. High values in Center City canyons produce architectural shading — a non-ecological cooling mechanism.
RVF — Road View Factor
Road/pavement pixel fraction. Street-level proxy for impervious heat storage. Complements satellite impervious % with a vertical perspective.
Canyon ratio (BVF ÷ SVF)
Building-to-sky ratio. Values >100 = deep H/W canyon. DK-205 = 1,124. Directly measures the radiative geometry that cools Center City grids despite high impervious %.
Canopy Height Proxy
Vegetation in upper ⅓ ÷ total vegetation pixels. Distinguishes tall mature canopy (Mt Airy 0.34) from low ground planting — critical for evapotranspiration efficiency.
Veg–Sky ratio (GVI ÷ SVF)
Vegetation density relative to sky openness. High values (W. Philly = 16.6) signal dense recent street planting — the morphological reinvestment signal.
Key finding
SVF shows the strongest single-variable correlation with |residual| (r = +0.279). Sky openness is what 2D satellite remote sensing cannot measure — and what predicts thermal anomalies best.
